Vision: Pluggable Ingestion Pipeline
Purpose

Provide creators with a zero-friction way to sync, schedule, and monitor content imports from their active media channels (YouTube, Spotify, Blog RSS) without manual copy-pasting.

Mechanic

The system automatically monitors the creator's configured content channels. When a new podcast, video, or article is published, the platform fetches the media in the background, transcribes the audio, identifies individual speakers, and updates the creator's library.

title: Vision: Verifiable AI Extraction
author: lhts1810
body:
lhts1810
opened
Vision: Verifiable AI Extraction
Purpose

Protect the creator's reputation by eliminating AI hallucinations, guaranteeing that every quote card or insight is 100% accurate and traceable back to the source media.

Mechanic

When the AI extracts key takeaways and quotes, a validation engine cross-references the quote word-for-word against the source transcript. If it passes verification, a deep link to the exact video/audio timestamp is created. Hallucinated or incorrect quotes are automatically rejected.

Surfaces affected
Console (Curation feed): Creator approval lists showing verbatim source highlights and mismatch warnings.
Embedded Widget: Audience citation badges that link directly to the source media.
V1 / V1.1 / Future split
V1: Verifying quote cards and key insights derived from a single episode context.
V1.1: AI self-correction prompts to automatically rewrite insights that fail verification.
Future: Synthesized insights that merge topics across multiple episodes.
Open decisions
How strict to make the automated matching algorithm (balancing small filler word variations vs. absolute verbatim correctness).
Scope exclusions
Allowing creators to create custom quote cards that cannot be verified in the transcript.

title: Vision: Controlled ingestion for large channels
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Purpose

A creator connects a channel with hundreds or thousands of videos and decides how much of it becomes Riffables — instead of the system blindly trying to ingest everything. They choose auto or manual up front, curate from a browsable catalog of their own videos, and trust that a big back-catalog never stalls the platform for everyone else.

"For a channel with many videos, you can't automatically crawl every video — the solution has to protect user UX and keep the system balanced."
— Hoc, product framing, 2026-07-08

Mechanic

Ingestion splits into cheap discovery (list a channel's videos, metadata only) and expensive processing (fetch → transcribe → extract). A creator picks an ingest mode when connecting: auto keeps today's behavior (latest and new videos flow in automatically), manual ingests nothing until they select from the catalog. Either way the channel's videos appear in a browsable, searchable catalog showing each video's ingest state. Selected or automatic work runs as bounded batches so progress is live, a run can be canceled mid-flight, and a whole-channel backfill yields to a fresh upload rather than blocking it.

Connect channel  →  pick mode (auto | manual)  →  catalog fills (metadata only, no spend)
   auto   → latest videos ingest now; new uploads picked up on refresh
   manual → nothing ingests until the creator selects videos from the catalog
Selected / automatic videos  →  processed in bounded batches
   → live progress · cancelable · large backfills never block another channel's fresh video

Surfaces affected

At connect, the creator sees a crawl-mode choice (auto vs manual) with a one-line explanation of each. Every connected channel gains a video catalog — a searchable list of its videos with publish dates and a per-video status (not ingested / queued / processing / riffed / failed). Creators select videos and start ingest for exactly those, watch progress advance live (videos done / total), and cancel a run in progress. A manual-mode channel hides the auto Run/Backfill controls; the catalog is its only ingest path. Mode is switchable later from the source card. Across the platform, one creator's large backfill no longer makes another creator's new video wait.

V1 / V1.1 / Future
V1 (shipped)
Connect a YouTube channel via ownership-verified Google OAuth
Auto-crawl the latest N videos on connect; manual Run / Backfill; ownership re-verify
Poll a crawl's status to completion
V1.1 (this Vision)
Auto vs manual ingest mode, chosen at connect and switchable later
Browsable, searchable per-channel video catalog with per-video ingest state (metadata only, no spend to browse)
Curate: select specific videos and ingest just those
Live incremental crawl progress; cancel a running crawl
Fair processing: a whole-channel backfill runs in bounded batches and never blocks another channel's fresh video
Future
Sort / filter the catalog by views and duration; show an estimated cost/time before ingesting a large selection
Automatic ingest policy for newly published videos beyond latest-N
Per-plan priority tiers for processing
Catalogs for non-YouTube sources (Spotify shows, blogs)
Detect videos that became private/deleted since they were cataloged
Open decisions
Milestone of the fairness work. One channel's backfill starving every other creator's fresh video is a defect in the shipped V1, not just a V1.1 nicety — the "fair processing" Epic may warrant pulling into a V1 patch rather than waiting for V1.1.
Default mode at connect — Auto (instant value, current behavior) vs Manual (control-first). Positioning call.
Does manual mode still offer a "backfill everything" shortcut, or must the creator always select from the catalog (even select-all)?
Catalog ceiling — how many of the newest videos to enumerate before the catalog is shown as "partial".
Out of scope
Making full-channel ingestion cheaper — this Vision spreads the cost over time and puts it behind explicit consent; it does not reduce per-video transcription/extraction cost.
Transcript quality / spoken-language selection — a separate ingestion concern.
Curation catalogs for non-YouTube sources — YouTube channels only in V1.1.
Refinement notes

(lead engineer fills during decomposition) — Spawned Epics: Ingest mode selection (V1.1) · Channel video catalog & curation (V1.1) · Crawl progress, cancellation & fair processing (V1.1, candidate V1 patch).

Source
PRD: PRD.md § Pillar A — Pluggable Ingestion Pipeline
Implementation plan: plan-channel-catalog.md (riffables-monorepo, branch feat/channel-catalog-crawl)

title: Epic: Ingest mode selection (auto vs manual)
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Parent Vision

Vision: Controlled ingestion for large channels (#18)

Milestone

V1.1

Mechanic

When connecting a channel the creator chooses how it ingests: auto (the platform pulls the latest videos now and new uploads later) or manual (nothing ingests until the creator selects videos from the catalog). The choice is enforced everywhere ingestion can start, so a manual channel can never spend transcription/extraction budget without an explicit selection. The mode is switchable later, and switching is a pure setting change — it neither starts nor stops any ingest.

Connect channel → [ Auto ]  latest videos ingest now; schedules + Run/Backfill active
                → [ Manual ] nothing ingests; catalog selection is the only path
Source card → switch Auto ⇄ Manual anytime (no ingest starts or stops on switch)

Modes
Mode	On connect	Run / Backfill / schedules	New uploads
Auto (default)	Latest videos ingest automatically	Available	Picked up automatically
Manual	Nothing ingests	Hidden / refused	Only when the creator selects them
Where chosen
Surface	Control
Connect-a-channel form	Auto / Manual picker with a one-line description of each
Source card	Mode badge + a switch action
Acceptance criteria
At connect the creator picks Auto or Manual; when they don't touch it, the channel is Auto.
A manual-mode channel ingests nothing on connect — no Riffs appear until a selection is made.
On a manual-mode channel the auto Run and Backfill controls are not shown; triggering them directly is refused with a clear, human-readable message.
An auto-mode channel behaves exactly as before this Epic (latest videos ingest on connect; Run/Backfill/schedules work).
The creator can switch a channel between Auto and Manual from the source card, and the new mode is reflected immediately.
Switching mode never starts or stops an ingest by itself: any in-flight crawl keeps running, and nothing new auto-fires.
Manual mode is offered only for channel sources; requesting it for a non-channel source is refused with a clear message.
Locked constants
Default mode: Auto (keeps existing creators/clients unchanged).
Manual mode is available for YouTube channels only (it is catalog-backed).
A mode switch takes effect at the next ingest decision; in-flight work is unaffected.
Backend dependencies
Existing ownership-verified channel connect (Google OAuth) — no new external dependency.
Out of scope
The catalog browser and selection flow — Channel video catalog & curation Epic.
Live progress and cancel — Crawl progress, cancellation & fair processing Epic.
Auto-confirm dialogs on switch (e.g. "cancel the running crawl too?") — deferred UX polish.

title: Epic: Channel video catalog & curation
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Parent Vision

Vision: Controlled ingestion for large channels (#18)

Milestone

V1.1

Mechanic

Every connected channel gains a catalog: a list of its videos discovered cheaply (metadata only — title, publish date, thumbnail — with no downloading or transcription). The creator browses and searches it, sees each video's ingest state, selects the videos worth turning into Riffables, and ingests exactly those. Browsing costs nothing; only an explicit selection spends processing budget.

Channel connected → catalog fills with the channel's videos (metadata only)
Creator → search / page → sees per-video state → ticks not-yet-ingested videos → Ingest selected
Refresh → new uploads appear; already-riffed videos are shown but not re-selectable

Catalog row
Field	Shown
Title	Video title (search matches this)
Published	Publish date
State	not ingested · queued · processing · riffed · failed
Per-video state
State	Meaning	Selectable
Not ingested	Never processed	Yes
Queued / Processing	In the pipeline now	No
Riffed	Already turned into Riffables	No
Failed	Last attempt failed	Yes (retry)
Actions
Action	Result
Refresh	Re-reads the channel; adds new uploads, keeps existing ones
Search	Filters the catalog by title
Select + Ingest	Starts ingest for exactly the ticked videos
Acceptance criteria
Connecting a channel populates a catalog of its videos (title + publish date) within a short time, without ingesting any of them.
The catalog shows a total video count and indicates when it is partial (for very large channels).
Each video displays its ingest state: not ingested / queued / processing / riffed / failed.
The creator can search the catalog by title and page through the results.
The creator can select one or more not-yet-ingested videos and start ingest for exactly those.
Already-riffed videos appear in the catalog but cannot be re-selected (no duplicate spend).
A refresh adds newly published videos to the catalog without re-ingesting ones already known.
Attempting to ingest videos that do not belong to the channel is refused.
Locked constants
Catalog enumerates up to the ~2,000 newest videos; beyond that it is shown as partial.
Browsing the catalog performs no downloading or transcription — metadata only.
A single selection is capped at 500 videos per request (the run itself is still processed in batches).
Backend dependencies
The channel's existing ownership grant is reused to read its video list (no new creator action).
Out of scope
Sorting / filtering by views or duration, and cost/time estimates before ingest — Future.
Auto vs manual mode — Ingest mode selection Epic.
Progress, cancel, and batch fairness of the ingest a selection triggers — Crawl progress Epic.

title: Epic: Crawl progress, cancellation, and fair processing
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Parent Vision

Vision: Controlled ingestion for large channels (#18)

Milestone

V1.1 (candidate V1 patch — the cross-creator starvation it fixes affects shipped V1; see the Vision's Open decisions)

Mechanic

Every ingest — auto teaser, whole-channel backfill, or catalog selection — runs as a sequence of small bounded batches instead of one long job. Batch boundaries make progress visible live, let a run be canceled mid-flight, and let fresh, interactive work overtake a large backlog so one creator's full-channel history never blocks another creator's new video.

Ingest starts → batch 1 → batch 2 → … (bounded size each)
   progress advances per batch · Cancel stops within one batch
   ordering: interactive Run > scheduled > catalog selection > whole-channel backfill

Progress & control
Surface	Behavior
Progress	Videos done / total, advancing live without reload
Cancel	Stops the run within one batch; no further videos picked up
Per-source lock	Only the source being crawled is busy; other sources stay actionable
Fairness (behavior)
Situation	Guarantee
One channel's 1,000-video backfill is running	Another creator's freshly added video still starts processing, not stuck behind it
A service restart mid-backfill	The backfill resumes from where it was, not from zero
Re-running an ingest	Already-riffed videos are never re-processed
Acceptance criteria
While a crawl runs, the creator sees videos-done / total advancing without reloading the page.
The creator can cancel a running crawl; it stops within one processing batch and picks up no further videos.
Videos that were canceled before processing return to "not ingested" and can be selected again later.
A newly added/selected video for one creator begins processing without waiting for another creator's whole-channel backfill to finish.
A large backfill completes incrementally and, after a service restart, resumes rather than starting over.
Re-running an ingest never produces duplicate Riffables for videos already riffed.
The control that started a crawl is disabled only for that source until the crawl ends — other sources remain usable.
Locked constants
Default processing batch size: 10 videos.
A crawl idle beyond 30 minutes is treated as abandoned and no longer blocks new runs on that source.
Processing order (highest first): interactive Run → scheduled → catalog selection → whole-channel backfill.
Backend dependencies
Shared transcription / extraction processing queues — this Epic governs how work is ordered and paced across them.
Out of scope
Per-plan / SLA-tier priority — Future.
Reducing per-video transcription or extraction cost.
The catalog UI (Channel video catalog Epic) and the mode gate (Ingest mode Epic) themselves.

Goal

A creator connecting a YouTube channel chooses Auto or Manual ingest before the connection completes.

Why

The mode has to be captured at connect time because a channel's very first crawl fires the moment it connects — if the creator can't say "don't auto-ingest" before that, a manual choice comes too late for a large channel. This is the entry point that makes every downstream "manual means no surprise spend" guarantee possible.

On the connect-a-channel form the creator sees an Auto / Manual choice with a one-line description of each; Auto is preselected. Connecting with Manual selected results in a channel that ingests nothing until the creator later selects videos. Connecting with Auto behaves exactly as today.

Switching mode after connect (separate Task).
Enforcing the manual gate on Run/Backfill/schedules (separate Task).

Goal

A manual-mode channel ingests nothing except an explicit catalog selection — no teaser crawl, no Run, no Backfill, no scheduled crawl.

Why

"Manual" is only trustworthy if it holds at every place ingestion can start, including a schedule firing at 3am or a stray API call. A single unguarded path would silently spend budget the creator chose to withhold, breaking the core promise of the mode.

A manual-mode channel shows no Run/Backfill controls. A direct attempt to Run, Backfill, or a scheduled trigger is refused with a clear message pointing the creator to select videos from the catalog instead. Connecting the channel fired no crawl. Switching it back to Auto restores all of those.

The UI mode switch itself (separate Task).

Why

A confirmation dialog offering to cancel an in-flight crawl on switch (deferred polish).

phanhoc

Epic: Crawl progress, cancellation, and fair processing (#22)

Goal

A creator stops a running crawl, and it halts promptly without leaving videos stuck.

Why

Started the wrong backfill, or changed their mind mid-run — the creator needs an exit that doesn't force them to wait out a thousand videos. Crucially, videos that hadn't been processed yet must become selectable again, so cancel-then-recurate is a clean loop (the core manual-mode workflow).

Example of done

With a crawl running, the creator hits Cancel; it stops within one processing batch and picks up no more videos. Videos already done stay done; videos not yet processed return to "not ingested" and can be selected again. The source's Run control re-enables once the crawl ends.

Out of scope
Auto-canceling on a mode switch (deferred; switch is a separate, side-effect-free action).
Agent instructions

Plan: plan-channel-catalog.md Tasks 2–4 — cancel flips the job AND its un-started videos back to a re-selectable state (otherwise they show "Queued" forever); the in-flight batch drains.

title: Task: Large backfill runs in bounded batches without blocking fresh videos
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Parent Epic

Epic: Crawl progress, cancellation, and fair processing (#22)

Goal

A whole-channel backfill processes in bounded batches, ordered so a fresh video for another creator never waits behind it.

Why

Today a single large backfill can monopolize shared transcription/extraction and stall every other creator's new upload — a cross-tenant fairness defect. Bounding each run into batches and ordering interactive/fresh work ahead of bulk history is what keeps the platform responsive under one creator's 1,000-video history.

Example of done

While creator A's 1,000-video backfill runs, creator B connects a channel (or a schedule fires) and B's new video starts processing without waiting for A's backfill to finish. A's backfill still completes, incrementally, and after a service restart it resumes rather than starting from zero. No video is processed twice.

Out of scope
Per-plan SLA tiers (Future).
The cancel and progress surfaces (separate Tasks).
Agent instructions

Plan: plan-channel-catalog.md Tasks 1–6 — one crawl = many bounded batches; priority propagates through the whole pipeline (interactive > scheduled > selection > backfill); heartbeat-based liveness. Deploy order: API before workers (see the plan's Backward compatibility section).

Source

RIFFABLES TEST PLAN
Information	Details
Project	Riffables AI
Doc type	Test Plan
Version	1.0
Author	QA
Objective	Fully cover the functionality based on the current PRD/Vision
1. References
https://github.com/speedrun-labs/riffables-prd/issues
2. Test Plan Objectives
Define the full testing scope for Riffables V1 based on the PRD.
Break down the functionality into clear modules.
Determine the test priority for each module: P0, P1, P2.
Identify the types of testing to be performed: Functional, Integration, API, UI, Security, AI Validation, and Regression.
Prioritize automation for stable flows with high regression value.
Leverage AI to accelerate test design, generate test data, support automation, and analyze defects.
3. Product Summary for Testing

Riffables is an AI platform that helps Creators turn content such as YouTube videos, Spotify podcasts, and RSS/Blogs into an AI Knowledge Base. The Audience can ask questions through a widget and receive answers with supporting sources, timestamps, and citations.

User Group	Purpose of Use	QA Scope to Focus On
Creator	Register an account, connect content sources, review/approve insights, customize the widget, and embed the widget.	Console/Dashboard, OAuth, sync status, review flow, theme builder.
Audience	Ask AI questions through the widget, view results, and open citations/timestamps.	Widget, search, result, citation, responsive.
Admin/Internal Team	Manage tenants, roles, support/debug.	Role, permission, tenant isolation, log visibility if available.
4. Scope of Testing
4.1 In Scope
Authentication and account setup.
Multi-tenant isolation and role permissions.
Creator Dashboard/Console.
Content source integration: YouTube, Spotify, RSS.
OAuth/Google/YouTube permission flow, including the recording flow required for Google verification if it is included in the app.
Ingestion Pipeline: detect, sync, download/extract, transcribe, embed, status, retry.
AI Insight/Quote generation.
Verification/Citation: verify transcript, timestamp, and deep link.
Hybrid Search: keyword + semantic + merge/ranking.
Creator Dashboard: content list, processing status, review/approve, theme configuration.
Audience Widget: question/search, answer, citation badge/link, responsive behavior.
Audience Widget: chat/search/result/citation/responsive behavior.
Widget Theme Customization using AI/chat, if included in V1.
Regression, smoke, API, UI, integration, and basic security testing.
4.2 Out of Scope
Livestream realtime.
Marketplace templates/themes.
Dedicated server/database for enterprise customers.
PDF, Google Docs, and PowerPoint if they are not included in V1.
Future recommendation engine.
Large-scale performance/load testing if there is no environment and no official benchmark requirement yet.
5. Module Coverage Matrix
Module	Coverage Area	Priority	Manual	Automation / AI Support
Authentication	Register, login, logout, session/token, invalid credentials.	P0	Yes	API + UI automation.
Multi-tenant & Permission	Tenant isolation, RBAC, direct URL/API access, token misuse.	P0	Yes	API automation + AI generates permission cases.
Content Integration	Paste YouTube/RSS URL, validate source, register feed, schedule automatic import job.	P0	Yes	API + UI automation; AI generates URL test data.
Ingestion Pipeline	Queued/Processing/Ready states, background import, retry, duplicate handling, timeout.	P0	Yes	API automation; AI analyzes job logs.
Transcription	Large audio <=180 minutes, raw transcript, speaker separation host/guest.	P0	Yes	API + manual review; AI supports transcript/speaker review.
Verification & Citation	Verbatim quote match, hallucination reject, mismatch warning, transcript highlight, exact timestamp link, server-generated link validity, citation performance.	P0	Yes	API automation + UI citation click; AI grounding/citation review.
Hybrid Search	Keyword search, semantic/concept search, quoted exact search, fused ranking/RRF, max 10 results, sub-second load, tenant-scoped results, insight card + verbatim quote display.	P0	Yes	API + UI automation; AI generates query variants and evaluates relevance.
Creator Dashboard	Content list, status indicators, review/approve/reject, errors, responsiveness.	P1	Yes	UI automation; AI screenshot/log support.
Audience Widget	Ask/search, result layout, citation click, responsive, API timeout.	P1	Yes	Playwright; AI generates UI scenarios.
Widget Theme Builder	Natural language theme requests, AI Builder theme assistant, instant preview simulator, save styles to creator profile, allowed CSS variables, malicious script refusal.	P1	Yes	UI automation; AI generates theme prompt variants; security negative prompt generation.
Self-Serve Re-extraction	Manual extraction on single content, re-extract pending queue, replace previous insights, role restriction, real-time status, batch limit safeguard.	P0	Yes	API automation + UI status validation; AI supports extraction log analysis and duplicate insight checks.
6. Test Types
Test Type	Objective	Applicable Modules
Functional Test	Verify that the functionality works according to the requirements.	All modules.
API Test	Validate business logic, permissions, and data responses.	Auth, Tenant, Search, Verification, Pipeline.
UI Test	Verify user flows and UI states.	Dashboard, Widget, Theme.
Integration Test	Verify the flow from source → pipeline → transcript → search.	Content Integration, Pipeline, AI Processing.
AI Validation Test	Evaluate grounding, hallucination, and citation accuracy.	AI Insight, Verification, Search.
Security Test	Test roles, tenant isolation, and tokens.	Tenant, API, Widget.
Performance	Verify latency/responsiveness and background jobs.	Search, Ingestion, Dashboard.
Regression Test	Ensure that fixes/new features do not break core flows.	P0/P1 modules.
Smoke Test	Quickly verify that the build is testable.	Login, Dashboard, Search, Widget, Citation.
7. Automation & AI Support

The project direction is to prioritize automated testing combined with AI agents to support the full QA lifecycle: reading the PRD, updating the Test Plan, generating checklists/test cases, generating or updating automation scripts, running tests, analyzing failures, and logging bugs.

Playwright acts as the test execution tool, while AI acts as an assistant for analysis, content generation, coordination, and result analysis. QA still reviews and approves the outputs before they are officially used.

Phase	AI + Playwright Usage	Output	Human Control
1. PRD/Epic Intake	AI reads the Vision/Epic/Acceptance Criteria, summarizes the changes, and identifies impacted modules.	Impact summary, updated coverage proposal.	QA review.
2. Test Plan Update	AI suggests updates to the Scope, Module Coverage, Test Data, Risks, and Quality Gates.	Draft Test Plan update.	QA approve.
3. Test Case/Checklist Design	AI generates checklists/test cases at the positive, negative, boundary, permission, and error-handling levels.	Draft checklist/test cases.	QA refine.
4. Playwright Test Generation	AI generates Playwright test skeletons, locators, fixtures, and API helpers if needed.	Automation draft / PR.	QA review code.
5. Automated Execution	The AI agent can run Playwright tests by suite or impacted area.	Execution report, trace, screenshot, video.	QA monitor.
6. Failure Analysis	AI reads logs, traces, screenshots, and network responses to classify failures.	Root cause suggestion.	QA verify.
7. Bug Logging	AI creates bug drafts including steps to reproduce, expected result, actual result, evidence, and severity suggestion.	Bug draft in tracker.	QA submit.
8. Regression Update	AI suggests additional regression cases based on new bugs or Epic updates.	Regression update proposal.	QA approve.
Control Rules for AI-generated Tests
AI-generated Test Plan changes must be reviewed by QA before delivery.
AI-generated test cases must be checked against PRD/Epic acceptance criteria before execution.
AI-generated Playwright scripts must pass code review before being merged into the automation repository.
AI may suggest severity and root cause, but QA/QA Lead owns the final bug classification.
AI may execute or trigger tests, but release Go/No-Go remains a human decision.
Sensitive tokens, cookies, private keys, production credentials and customer data must not be pasted into AI prompts.
8. Automation Coverage Matrix
Module	Manual	API Auto	UI Auto	AI Support
Authentication	Yes	Yes	Yes	Generate scripts and negative credential test cases.
Multi-tenant & Permission	Yes	Yes	Partial	Generate permission/direct URL test cases.
Content Integration	Yes	Yes	Yes	Generate valid/invalid/duplicate URL test cases.
Ingestion Pipeline	Yes	Yes	Partial	Analyze job logs, retry patterns, and timeout patterns.
Transcription/Speaker Separation	Yes	Partial	No	AI supports transcript/speaker review.
Verification & Citation	Yes	Yes	Partial	Evaluate grounding and citation accuracy.
Hybrid Search	Yes	Yes	Yes	Generate keyword, semantic, and quoted query variants; AI evaluates relevance and top-result quality.
Dashboard	Yes	Partial	Yes	Generate locators and review screenshots.
Audience Widget	Yes	Partial	Yes	Generate UI scenarios.
Widget Theme Builder	Yes	Partial	Yes	Generate prompt variants, invalid/malicious prompts, review preview screenshots, and saved style configuration.
Self-Serve Re-extraction	✓	✓	✓	AI analyzes extraction logs, duplicate insight behavior, and status failure patterns.
AI + Playwright Workflow	✓	✓	✓	AI reads PRD/Epic documents, generates test cases, generates/updates Playwright scripts, runs tests, analyzes traces/logs, and creates bug drafts.
9. Entry Criteria / Exit Criteria
Entry Criteria	Exit Criteria
PRD/Vision/Epic is clear enough to define the testing scope.	100% P0 test executed.
Build has been successfully deployed.	No Critical bugs remain.
Test accounts, source data, and OAuth test app are ready.	High-priority bugs have been fixed or approved for deferral.
Key API endpoints or main UI flows are accessible.	Regression smoke tests have passed, and 100% of AI-assisted findings and scripts have been reviewed by QA.
Test data/golden dataset has been prepared.	P0 citation accuracy reaches 100%.
10. Proposed Test Plan
Step	AI Role	QA Role	Output	Estimate
AI Tool Usage Research	x	AI Tool Research	Operate the Playwright AI Agent to support testing.	6h
Read PRD	Summarize requirements, Acceptance Criteria, and risks.	Review.	Requirement summary.	2h
Update Test Plan	Suggest scope, coverage, and risks.	Approve/update.	Updated Test Plan.	1h
Generate Test Cases	Generate checklists/test cases.	Review/refine.	Test cases.	TBD
Generate Automation	Generate scripts based on test cases.	Refactor/run.	Automation script.	TBD
Execute Tests	Analyze traces/logs/screenshots and run automation.	Perform manual testing and confirm issues.	Test result.	TBD
Log Bug	Generate bug title, steps, actual result, and expected result.	Submit bug.	Bug report.	TBD
11. QA Deliverables
Test Plan.
Module checklist.
Test cases.
Automation scripts.
Bug report.
Regression report.
Test summary report.
12. Release Quality Gate
Metric	Target	Release Decision
Critical bug	0	No-Go if any remain.
P0 test pass rate	100%	No-Go if not achieved.
Citation accuracy on P0 dataset	100%	No-Go if not achieved.
Tenant leak	0	No-Go if any occur.
Smoke test	Pass	No-Go if failed.
Automation regression pass rate	>=95%	Review if lower.
Search relevance on golden dataset	>=95% if benchmark is available.	Review if lower.
AI-generated automation review	100% AI-generated Playwright scripts reviewed before merge.	Unreviewed AI-generated test code used in CI.
AI bug draft governance	QA verifies all AI-generated bug reports before submission.	AI-generated bug submitted without QA validation.

author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3

Vision: Creator Console Dashboard (#15)

Milestone

V1.1

Mechanic

A live audit of the console (Home, Sources, Content, Sites, Editor) found the accessibility baseline is already solid — see "Verified passing" below. This epic closes the handful of real gaps that remain: sub-minimum tap targets, a missing success affordance after publishing, and a thin heading structure for screen-reader navigation. Post-V1 polish, not launch-blocking.

Verified passing (audit, 2026-07-09)
Colour contrast meets WCAG AA on Home/Content/Editor (muted greys included).

Forms are labelled; empty, loading, and error states exist (errors use role="alert").

Acceptance criteria
Every interactive control presents a pointer target of at least 24×24px (icon buttons in the editor toolbar and content rows included), per WCAG 2.2 AA 2.5.8.
Immediately after a successful publish, the operator can open the live site in one click (from the editor, not only from /sites).
Each dashboard screen and the editor expose a heading outline a screen reader can navigate (not H1-only / heading-less).
Out of scope
The mobile/tablet story — see open question.
Open questions
Mobile/tablet: the editor already gates gracefully below lg (a clear "Open the editor on a larger screen" message + Back to Sites link — verified), and the dashboard screens work on mobile. The remaining question is narrower: should the onboarding tours run on mobile at all, or is desktop-only fine for the target operator? Needs a product call before scoping.

title: Task: Guard against template styles.css drifting from source
author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3
opened
Parent Epic

Epic: Console UX & accessibility hardening (#45)

Goal

The template's compiled styles.css can't silently drift from the components it's built from.

Why

The template ships a pre-compiled packages/templates/creator-library/src/styles.css (built via build:css from source). It's regenerated by hand, so when someone edits a component's classNames and forgets to rebuild, classes the component uses silently vanish from the shipped CSS — the element just loses that styling with no error. This already bit us twice in SiteHeader: px-block (visible — the desktop header lost all horizontal padding) and lg:hidden (latent), both fixed in riffables-monorepo #27.

Example of done

CI fails the build if styles.css is out of date with source — e.g. it runs build:css and git diff --exit-code on the output, so a drifted stylesheet can't be merged. Caveat to handle: classes applied dynamically (via Puck props / manifest, not literal classNames) aren't caught by Tailwind's content scan, so the guard must pair with a safelist for those rather than blindly pruning them.

Out of scope

Migrating the template off a committed compiled stylesheet (bigger architectural change).

Source
Parent Epic: Epic: Console UX & accessibility hardening #45

author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3

Vision: Creator Console Dashboard (#15)

Milestone

V1.1

Mechanic

A backend↔frontend audit (2026-07-10) cross-referenced every service-api endpoint against what the console actually calls. service-api is broadly implemented, but several working capabilities are not wired into the console UI — the operator can't reach them. This epic closes those gaps.

Gaps found (backend has it, console doesn't use it)
Capability	Backend	Console today
Hybrid search	GET /search (semantic + keyword + RRF)	Content search box filters client-side only (substring over loaded rows)
Recurring schedules	full CRUD /schedules	not wired — creators can't set a crawl schedule (a stated V1 need)
Site lifecycle	unpublish / discard-draft / delete	only publish is wired
Article editing	GET/PATCH /articles/:id	client fns exist but dead — publish/reject only, no edit
Job/queue status	GET /jobs/status	not wired — no ops visibility
Acceptance criteria
Content search returns backend hybrid-search results, not a client-side substring filter.
An operator can create and edit recurring crawl schedules from the console.
An operator can unpublish, discard draft changes on, and delete a site.
An operator can edit an article's content, not only publish/reject it.
Job/queue status is visible somewhere in the console.
The orphan /sites/temp and /sites/configuration routes (hardcoded fake site names) are gone.
Open questions (product decisions, not tasks)
Widget-config module (/widget, /widget/templates, /widget/bundle, /widget/preview) is fully implemented in the backend but entirely unwired — the console ships the visual site builder instead. Formally deprecate/remove it, or keep for a future embed path? (Ties to the PRD v2 "drop the widget" direction.)
Editor AI Assistant (POST /agent/chat + SSE) is built end-to-end but hard-gated off (ASSISTANT_ENABLED = false). Ship it or remove it?
Out of scope
The audience-site data-honesty items (synthetic guest/show labels, chat stub) — tracked separately under Vision Vision: Public Audience Site #16.

author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3

Epic: Console — wire the remaining backend APIs (#51)

Goal

The Content search box uses the backend hybrid search instead of a client-side substring filter.

Why

service-api exposes GET /search (semantic kNN + full-text, fused with RRF), but the console's Content panel searches with a local filterRiffs — a case-insensitive substring match over only the rows already loaded. So search is shallow (misses semantic matches and anything not yet fetched) and doesn't use the discovery engine that was built.

Typing in the Content search calls GET /search and shows ranked hybrid results across the tenant's content, not just substring matches on the current page.

Public-site audience search (already wired to /public/search).

title: Task: Operator takes a published site offline with a distinct 'unpublished' state
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Task: Operator takes a published site offline with a distinct "unpublished" state
Source
Parent: Vision: Public Audience Site (Issue Vision: Public Audience Site #16)
Goal

An operator can take their published audience site offline, and the console shows it as unpublished — clearly distinct from a draft that was never published. Bringing the site back is one action (publish again) and restores exactly what was live before.

Why

Unpublishing today silently reverts the site to "draft", so the site list cannot tell "taken down, ready to re-launch" apart from "never launched". Operators who temporarily pull a site (content fixes, rebranding, legal takedown) lose the signal that this site had a live audience and can be restored as-is. The distinction matters for the dashboard, for support, and for the operator's own confidence that nothing was lost.

Example of done
Unpublishing a published site flips its status to unpublished; visitors on its domain get "site not found" immediately.
The site keeps its content, pending edits, and domain while offline; the domain stays reserved (nobody else can claim it).
Publishing again restores the exact previous live version at the same domain.
Unpublishing a site that is not live does nothing (no error, no state change) — the action is safe to repeat.
A site can never be created directly in the unpublished state; the only way in is taking a published site down.
The console/site list shows the three states distinctly: draft, published, unpublished.
Scope exclusions
Filtering the site list by status.
Recording when a site was unpublished (audit timestamp).
Any change to how the audience site itself renders.
Agent instructions

Design spec (approved): docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-10-site-unpublished-status-design.md in riffables-monorepo — state machine, decisions (transition-only status, idempotent unpublish, no DB migration needed), file-level change list, and test matrix live there.

title: Bug: backend semantic/hybrid search returns 500 (keyword works)
author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3
opened
Parent Epic

Epic: Console — wire the remaining backend APIs (#51)

Summary

GET /api/search returns 500 for any non-empty query in mode=semantic and mode=hybrid. mode=keyword works. Since hybrid fuses semantic + keyword, the whole hybrid discovery engine is unusable until this is fixed.

Evidence (2026-07-10, against the deployed api.riffables.com)
mode	result
keyword	✅ 200 — ranked full-text hits with highlighted snippets, correct via: ["keyword"]
semantic	❌ 500 Internal server error
hybrid	❌ 500 (inherits the semantic failure)
empty q	✅ 200 []

So the fault is in the semantic path (query embedding → pgvector kNN → RRF fusion), not the FTS path. Likely candidates: the query embedder (OpenAI call / deterministic-fake-embedding dimension vs the stored chunk embeddings), or the pgvector kNN query.

Impact
Audience/public discovery (Pillar C) and the console Content search only get keyword matching, no semantic relevance.
The console Content search (prd Task: Use the backend hybrid search instead of client-side filtering #52) already wired to prefer hybrid and auto-fall-back to keyword, so it works today — it will silently upgrade to semantic+keyword the moment this is fixed. No FE change needed after the fix.
Example of done

GET /api/search?q=…&mode=hybrid returns 200 with fused semantic+keyword hits (via may include "semantic"), for both ASCII and non-ASCII (e.g. Vietnamese) queries.

Source
Parent Epic: Epic: Console — wire the remaining backend APIs #51 (surfaced while wiring Task: Use the backend hybrid search instead of client-side filtering #52)
Discovery engine: docs / PRD Pillar C.

title: Task: Implement Editor Cross-Panel Highlighting
author: lhts1810
body:
lhts1810
opened
Task: Implement Editor Cross-Panel Highlighting
Source
Parent: Vision: Creator Console Dashboard (Issue Vision: Creator Console Dashboard #15)
Goal

Implement synchronized cross-panel hover states in the site editor. Hovering over a component in a sidebar panel must visually highlight its corresponding element on the live Canvas (and vice versa).

Why

Currently, the editor panels operate independently without direct visual mapping. Creators struggle to understand which settings or structural items map to which visual blocks on the live site preview. Providing immediate hover-state feedback creates a tighter visual connection, making the editor much more intuitive and faster to navigate.

Example of done
Hovering over a component entry in a sidebar panel applies a visual highlight (e.g., a colored border, outline, or light overlay) to that exact component rendered on the center Canvas.
Conversely, hovering over a visual block directly on the center Canvas highlights its corresponding entry/settings in the sidebar panels.
The visual highlights apply instantly on hover without noticeable lag.
Scope exclusions
Building drag-and-drop structural reordering functionality (this task is strictly about visual hover mapping).
Changing the underlying CSS theme tokens or the HTML structure of the generated public site.

title: Task: Link inspector fields to their text on the canvas (hover/focus highlight)
author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3
opened
Parent Epic

Epic: Visual site builder — bind sections to live catalog data (#35)

Goal

Hovering or focusing an inspector field highlights the text it controls on the canvas.

Why

In a fixed section with many inspector fields (Hero has eyebrow / headline / tagline / CTA labels / …), it's not obvious which field maps to which bit of the rendered page. A live field↔element link (like Webflow) removes the guesswork while editing.

Example of done

Hovering — or focusing — a text field in the inspector outlines the matching text in the selected section on the canvas; focus also scrolls it into view. It works without per-field template annotation (value/placeholder is matched against the section's rendered text), and URL/image fields that have no visible text simply don't highlight.

Out of scope

Highlighting text that comes from live backend data (a section rendering a fetched riffable) — the field value won't match dynamic content.

title: Task: Conversationally edit the public site with the builder agent (wire the site-editor Assistant)
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Parent Vision

Vision: Conversational Customization (#9)
Sibling: Epic: Conversational Widget Theme Customizer (#5) — this is the same conversational-customization mechanic applied to the public site (the Puck editor) instead of the embeddable widget.

Goal

An operator editing their public site in the console can describe a change in plain language ("add a call-to-action at the bottom", "rewrite the hero headline", "show my latest Riffables") and the site-editor Assistant proposes it as a previewable diff they accept — no manual section wrangling.

Why

The site editor already ships an Assistant panel (apps/console/src/components/app/editor/assistant-panel.tsx), but it is a design-only stub: ASSISTANT_ENABLED = false and every prompt returns a hardcoded draftReply() ("Design preview — not yet connected to a model", per ADR 0008). The builder agent behind it (service-agent builder profile) only knows the widget flow — there are no site MCP tools and no wiring from the editor to the agent. So the most prominent affordance in the site editor does nothing.

Example of done

In /sites/editor, typing a request into the Assistant streams a real response from the builder agent, scoped to the operator's tenant + site, and renders a proposal the operator can Preview on the canvas and Apply (or Revert). Applied changes persist to the site draft and survive reload. The agent never writes the site directly and never publishes — the operator stays in control.

Mechanic (locked in design)
Read via MCP, apply client-side (ADR 0008): new builder-mode read tools get_site_draft + describe_site_components expose the current draft + editable section types; the agent proposes a structured mutation-ops block (insert_section / set_props / move_section / remove_section); a deterministic console engine validates each op against the component manifest and applies it to the Puck tree.
Shadow Preview → confirmed Apply → draft persist. Publish stays a manual operator action.
Tenant + operator scoping is enforced by the signed MCP token (adds userId); site tools are read-only and builder-mode only.
Out of scope
Agent creating a site from scratch, auto-publishing, or multi-route / navigation restructuring (ops target the currently-open route only).
The ask_user interactive round-trip in the panel (fast-follow after the one-shot propose/apply path is verified).
New template packages / component authoring.
The audience-side chat (that is Task: Wire the real audience chat (replace the Echo stub) #60) and the widget theme customizer (Epic: Conversational Widget Theme Customizer #5).
Source
Design spec + implementation plan in the monorepo: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-13-agent-site-builder-design.md, docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-13-agent-site-builder.md (branch feat/agent-site-builder).
Backend↔FE review of the site editor Assistant stub (ADR 0008).

title: Task: Ask first-time users before auto-running onboarding tours
author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3
opened
Source
Parent: Vision: Creator Console Dashboard (Vision: Creator Console Dashboard #15)
Related: the open question on Epic Epic: Console UX & accessibility hardening #45 (when should onboarding tours run).
Goal

After the first successful sign-in, the console asks whether the user is new and only auto-runs the per-screen onboarding tours for users who say yes.

Why

Tours used to auto-start unconditionally on each screen's first visit. A returning operator (new browser, cleared storage, second machine) got walked through screens they already know — the question puts the choice with the user, once.

Example of done
On the first authenticated visit (per user, tracked in localStorage) a dialog asks "First time in Riffables?" with I'm new — show me around (tours auto-start as before, current screen immediately) and I've been here before (no auto-start ever; the floating Help button still replays any tour).
Dismissing without answering persists nothing and re-offers next visit.
Users who already completed the home tour are grandfathered — never asked.
Desktop-only, matching the tours themselves; the focus-mode editor honors the same choice.

title: Task: [backend] Wire BetterAuth Google social provider (socialProviders.google) for operator login
author: phanhoc
body:
phanhoc
opened
Source
Parent: Vision: Creator Console Dashboard (Vision: Creator Console Dashboard #15)
Backend counterpart of Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70 (infra: register the Google callback). Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70 says the code is done in monorepo 397653a, but that commit is not in origin/main (tip d05a4b5 has no socialProviders in packages/auth). This task lands that backend wiring into main so Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70 becomes actionable.
Goal

BetterAuth on service-api enables Google social sign-in for operators — the missing backend half behind the (already-present but inert) "Continue with Google" button.

Why

The console already renders a "Continue with Google" button (apps/console/src/components/auth/auth-panel.tsx, GoogleMark), and #70 tracks the Google Cloud redirect-URI registration. But origin/main has no socialProviders.google — so there's nothing for the button or the callback to hit. Without this, Google login can't work regardless of infra.

Scope (backend only)
packages/auth/src/auth.ts: add optional googleLogin?: { clientId; clientSecret } to CreateAuthOptions. When present → socialProviders.google with scopes openid email profile. Always set account.accountLinking = { enabled: true, trustedProviders: ["google"] } (auto-link a Google identity to an existing email/password user of the same email — Google has verified it).
service-api/src/modules/auth/auth.module.ts: pass googleLogin from the existing GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID / GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET config — reuse the same Google OAuth app as YouTube ownership (per Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70: no new Vault secrets). Only wire when both are set.
Decisions (aligned with #70)
Reuse the existing Google OAuth client, not a dedicated one — no new secrets. Login scopes are per-request (openid email profile), independent of the YouTube flow's youtube.readonly.
Backend-native callback /api/auth/callback/google off BETTER_AUTH_URL; session shared via BETTER_AUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN (.speedrunlabs.ai). No console-proxy changes.
No Prisma changes — BetterAuth's account table stores the social identity.
Does not touch the YouTube-OAuth code, the console UI, or GOOGLE_OAUTH_REDIRECT_URI (that points at the source callback).
Out of scope (tracked elsewhere)
Wiring the inert console button to sign-in/social — frontend task.
Registering the callback in Google Cloud / Vault secret check — Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70.
Example of done
createAuth({ googleLogin: { clientId, clientSecret } }) produces options.socialProviders.google (scope openid email profile) and options.account.accountLinking.trustedProviders includes google; absent creds → no google provider (unit-tested in service-api).
With the existing GOOGLE_OAUTH_* set, GET /api/auth/callback/google is live on service-api.
After Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70 registers the redirect URI and the button is wired: a new Google user lands on /setup-organization; an existing-email operator is linked to their existing account (no duplicate user).
Design doc

docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-14-google-oauth-login-design.md (riffables-monorepo).

Create sub-issue

title: Task: [frontend] Wire the Continue-with-Google button to BetterAuth social sign-in
author: sunday19x3
body:
sunday19x3
opened
Source
Parent: Vision: Creator Console Dashboard (Vision: Creator Console Dashboard #15)
Backend counterpart: Task: [backend] Wire BetterAuth Google social provider (socialProviders.google) for operator login #71 (socialProviders.google) · Infra: Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70 (redirect URI)
Goal

The console's "Continue with Google" button starts a real Google sign-in instead of being decorative.

Why

The button (auth-panel.tsx) rendered with no onClick — inert. With the backend Google provider wired (#41), the frontend just needed to call the social route and follow the redirect. Two issues surfaced while wiring it: the auth proxy 404'd the social route, and the cross-root-domain cookie problem (console speedrunlabs.ai vs API riffables.com) meant the backend-native callback couldn't set a usable session cookie.

What shipped (riffables-monorepo main)
a7902db — signInWithGoogle() posts /api/auth/sign-in/social and follows the returned consent URL; the button gets pending state + inline error (404 → "isn't available on this server yet").
80057b0 — proxy fix: the console auth proxy's POST allowlist didn't include sign-in/social, so the proxy itself 404'd the request before it reached the backend. Added it.
5cf9c6f (+ devops 047acd0 Dockerfile) — pointed the console/public-site API base at api-riffables.speedrunlabs.ai. The backend moving under the speedrunlabs.ai root fixes the cookie problem: the backend-native Google callback can now set a valid .speedrunlabs.ai session cookie (previously api.riffables.com couldn't set a .speedrunlabs.ai cookie).
Example of done

Clicking "Continue with Google" on the deployed console redirects to Google's consent screen and lands back signed in (session cookie on .speedrunlabs.ai); a brand-new Google account routes to /setup-organization.

Remaining (not frontend)
Register the redirect URI https://api-riffables.speedrunlabs.ai/api/auth/callback/google on the Google OAuth app (infra — note the host changed from what Task: [infra] Register the BetterAuth Google callback so Continue-with-Google goes live #70 originally specified).
BETTER_AUTH_URL / BETTER_AUTH_TRUSTED_ORIGINS on the new host — already set in gitops.
Verified

Preview (unauthenticated): button renders, click posts /sign-in/social; flag-off/flag-removed states; lint + production build pass. Full round-trip needs the redirect URI registered + a real Google account.
